title: Independent and Objective Oversight of Ukrainian Assistance Act
short_title: Independent and Objective Oversight of Ukrainian Assistance Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a special office to watch over all the money and aid that America sends to Ukraine. The office will be run by a Special Inspector General who will check to make sure the money is being spent correctly. They will write reports every three months to tell Congress what they found and put these reports online in different languages so people can read them. The office will keep doing this job until the amount of money being sent to help rebuild Ukraine goes below 250 million dollars.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title; Table of Contents
sections.text: This law can be called the Independent and Objective Oversight of Ukrainian Assistance Act. The law contains thirteen sections that cover topics like the purpose of the law, definitions of key terms, creating a special inspector general office, the duties and powers of that office, reporting requirements, and when the law will end.

sections.title: Section 2 Purposes
sections.text: This law has three main goals. First, it will create independent and objective audits and investigations of programs and operations that are funded with money given to Ukraine for military, economic, and humanitarian aid. Second, it will provide independent leadership and recommendations for policies that make these programs run more efficiently and effectively while preventing and finding waste, fraud, and abuse. Third, it will create an independent way to keep the Secretary of State, Secretary of Defense, and heads of other relevant federal agencies fully informed about problems and needed fixes in these programs.

sections.title: Section 3 Definitions
sections.text: This section explains what certain terms mean in this law. Money appropriated or otherwise made available for military, economic, and humanitarian aid to Ukraine includes money for the Ukraine Security Assistance Initiative, Foreign Military Financing funding for Ukraine, certain Department of State programs, and money from specific parts of the Ukraine Supplemental Appropriations Act. Appropriate congressional committees means eight specific committees in the Senate and House of Representatives that deal with appropriations, armed services, foreign relations, homeland security, and oversight. Office means the Office of the Special Inspector General for Ukrainian Military, Economic, and Humanitarian Aid that this law creates. Special Inspector General means the person appointed to head this office.

sections.title: Section 4 Establishment of Office of the Special Inspector General for Ukrainian Military, Economic, and Humanitarian Aid
sections.text: This section creates a new office called the Office of the Special Inspector General for Ukrainian Military, Economic, and Humanitarian Aid. The President will appoint the head of this office, who must be chosen within thirty days after this law is passed. The person chosen must have integrity and proven ability in accounting, auditing, financial analysis, law, management analysis, public administration, or investigations. The Special Inspector General will be paid at the same rate as other high-level government officials. This person cannot participate in political activities and can be removed from office according to existing federal law.

sections.title: Section 5 Assistant Inspectors General
sections.text: The Special Inspector General must hire two assistant inspectors general following civil service laws and regulations. One Assistant Inspector General for Auditing will oversee auditing activities related to programs supported by money given to Ukraine for military, economic, and humanitarian aid. One Assistant Inspector General for Investigations will oversee investigative activities related to these same programs.

sections.title: Section 6 Supervision
sections.text: The Special Inspector General will report to and be supervised by both the Secretary of State and the Secretary of Defense. However, no officer from the Department of Defense, Department of State, United States Agency for International Development, or any other relevant federal agency can prevent or stop the Special Inspector General from starting, carrying out, or completing any audit or investigation related to money given to Ukraine for military, economic, and humanitarian aid. They also cannot prevent the Special Inspector General from issuing subpoenas during these audits or investigations.

sections.title: Section 7 Duties
sections.text: The Special Inspector General must conduct, supervise, and coordinate audits and investigations of how money appropriated for military, economic, and humanitarian aid to Ukraine is treated, handled, and spent. This includes overseeing how the money is obligated and spent, monitoring reconstruction activities and contracts funded by this money, reviewing how funds and information are transferred between government departments and private entities, keeping records for future audits, monitoring how effectively the United States coordinates with Ukraine and other countries, investigating overpayments and potential illegal actions by federal employees or contractors, and referring investigation reports to the Department of Justice when needed. The Special Inspector General must also establish appropriate systems and procedures to carry out these duties, consult with congressional committees before doing audits outside of Ukraine, and coordinate with other inspectors general from relevant federal agencies.

sections.title: Section 8 Powers and Authorities
sections.text: The Special Inspector General has the same authorities as other inspectors general under federal law, except they cannot audit or investigate the intelligence community. The Special Inspector General must follow federal audit standards when carrying out their duties. They can use expedited hiring authority to quickly hire employees, but this authority expires one hundred eighty days after this law is enacted or one hundred eighty days after the Special Inspector General is confirmed by the Senate, whichever is later. The Special Inspector General can also rehire up to twenty five retired federal employees and allow them to continue receiving their retirement benefits while working.

sections.title: Section 9 Personnel, Facilities, and Other Resources
sections.text: The Special Inspector General can hire officers and employees following federal civil service laws about competitive hiring and pay scales. They can also hire experts and consultants at daily rates not exceeding the rate for a GS-15 federal employee. The Special Inspector General can enter into contracts for audits, studies, and other services if Congress provides money for these activities. The Secretary of State or Secretary of Defense must provide the Special Inspector General with appropriate office space in Ukraine or European partner countries, necessary equipment and supplies, and maintenance services. Other federal agencies must provide information or assistance when requested by the Special Inspector General, and if they refuse, the Special Inspector General must immediately report this to the Secretary of State or Secretary of Defense and to the appropriate congressional committees.

sections.title: Section 10 Reports
sections.text: The Special Inspector General must submit quarterly reports to congressional committees, the Secretary of State, and the Secretary of Defense within thirty days after each quarter ends. These reports must summarize the Special Inspector General's activities and the activities of programs funded with money given to Ukraine for military, economic, and humanitarian aid. The reports must include detailed statements of all obligations, expenditures, and revenues, including money spent by different agencies, project costs, foreign contributions, seized assets, operating expenses, and information about major contracts and agreements. The Special Inspector General must publish these reports online in English, Ukrainian, and Russian. Reports should be unclassified but can include classified sections if necessary. The Secretary of State and Secretary of Defense can submit comments on these reports within thirty days.

sections.title: Section 11 Transparency
sections.text: The Secretary of State and Secretary of Defense must make copies of the Special Inspector General's reports available to the public upon request at a reasonable cost within sixty days of receiving them. They must also make their own comments available to the public in the same way. However, the President can waive this requirement for national security reasons. If the President uses this waiver, they must publish a notice in the Federal Register. Nothing in this section allows the public disclosure of information that is prohibited by other laws, protected by Executive order for national security reasons, or part of an ongoing criminal investigation.

sections.title: Section 12 Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: Congress is authorized to provide twenty million dollars for fiscal year two thousand twenty six to carry out this law. To pay for this, twenty million dollars is taken away from money previously appropriated for assistance to Europe, Eurasia, and Central Asia under the Ukraine Security Supplemental Appropriations Act of two thousand twenty four.

sections.title: Section 13 Termination
sections.text: The Office will end one hundred eighty days after the amount of unspent money appropriated for Ukraine reconstruction falls below two hundred fifty million dollars. Before the office ends, the Special Inspector General must prepare and submit a final forensic audit report to the appropriate congressional committees about programs and operations funded with money given to Ukraine for military, economic, and humanitarian aid.
